records.ety: [OE. sheld, scheld, AS. scield, scild, sceld, scyld; akin to OS. scild, OFries. skeld, D. & G. schild, OHG. scilt, Icel. skjöldr, Sw. sköld, Dan. skiold, Goth. skildus; of uncertain origin. Cf. Sheldrake.]

defs: A broad piece of defensive armor, carried on the arm, -- formerly in general use in war, for the protection of the body. See Buckler.
quotes: Now put your shields before your hearts and fight, With hearts more proof than shields.

defs: Anything which protects or defends; defense; shelter; protection.
Figuratively, one who protects or defends.
quotes: Fear not, Abram; I am thy shield, and thy exceeding great reward.

defs: In lichens, a Hardened cup or disk surrounded by a rim and containing the fructification, or asci.
The escutcheon or field on which are placed the bearings in coats of arms. Cf. Lozenge.
A framework used to protect workmen in making an adit under ground, and capable of being pushed along as excavation progresses.
A spot resembling, or having the form of, a shield.
A coin, the old French crown, or cu, having on one side the figure of a shield.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Shielded; p. pr. & vb. n. Shielding.]

defs: To cover with, or as with, a shield; to cover from danger; to defend; to protect from assault or injury.
quotes: Shouts of applause ran ringing through the field, To see the son the vanquished father shield.
A woman's shape doth shield thee.

defs: To ward off; to keep off or out.
quotes: They brought with them their usual weeds, fit to shield the cold to which they had been inured.

defs: To avert, as a misfortune; hence, as a supplicatory exclamation, forbid!
quotes: God shield that it should so befall.
God shield I should disturb devotion!
